title: Exception thrown inside run() finally block can suppress the original error
description: In run(), if feedDecoder or readDecoderEvents throws, the catch calls failSession. Then the finally block calls finishSession (which is idempotent) and then awaits disposeActiveDecoder(). disposeActiveDecoder() itself catches disposal errors, so that path is safe. However, finishSession() calls callbacks.onSessionEnd(), which is an external callback. If onSessionEnd throws a synchronous exception inside the finally block, that exception propagates out of run() and silently replaces the original error, changing what the caller's .catch sees. The original error that triggered failSession is lost.

title: Output size guard uses 1 byte/pixel minimum but does not account for multi-byte formats before allocating
description: The comment says the guard uses a conservative 1 byte/pixel minimum so any format that would exceed the cap is caught. However, the guard fires at 1 GiB / 1 byte = 1 billion pixels, which for rgba16 (8 bytes/pixel) is 8 GiB of actual output — well above the stated 1 GiB cap. A 16 384×16 384 rgba16 image is ~2 GiB; it passes the guard (1 byte/px = 256 MB < 1 GiB) but the actual WASM heap requirement is 2 GiB. The guard is not conservative for high-bpp formats. A crafted JPEG XL image with large dimensions and rgba16 format can make the WASM heap grow to multiple GiB before any OOM or explicit limit fires.

title: run() rejection caught by a lambda that calls failSession, but failSession itself can throw if callbacks.onSessionEnd throws
description: The constructor wires: this.run().catch((err) => this.failSession("Internal", String(err))). Inside failSession, finishSession() is called, which invokes this.callbacks.onSessionEnd(this.sessionId). If that callback throws a synchronous exception, it propagates out of failSession, out of the .catch callback, and becomes an unhandled promise rejection — there is no outer try/catch. In worker.ts the onSessionEnd is (sessionId) => decodeSessions.delete(sessionId), which cannot throw, so this is low-risk in the current wiring, but the type system does not enforce that contract and a future callback change can silently produce an unhandled rejection.

title: EncodeHandler constructor .catch calls failSession which itself may be in terminal state from run()'s finally, silently losing the error
description: The constructor does this.run().catch((err) => { this.failSession("Internal", message); }). Inside run()'s finally, finishSession is called. If run() throws and the finally finishSession succeeds, then the .catch-level failSession call hits the isTerminal() guard and silently returns without posting any error message. This means if run() throws after the session was already marked terminal in the finally block, the original error string is completely discarded with no trace. The current flow is: throw → catch(failSession) → isTerminal()=true → no-op. The original error is only logged if run() itself calls failSession before throwing.

title: feedDecoder continues calling decoder.push after disposeActiveDecoder may have nulled the decoder reference during concurrent cancel
description: feedDecoder receives a local `decoder` parameter and calls decoder.push() in a loop. Meanwhile, onCancel() calls disposeActiveDecoder() which calls decoder.dispose(). Because the local reference in feedDecoder is captured from the parameter — not from this.decoder — it remains valid after this.decoder = null, so push() is called on a disposed decoder. Whether this causes an error depends on the WASM facade's behavior after dispose, but it is at minimum an ordering hazard. The cancel path sets this.cancelled = true and calls this.wake() which unblocks waitForChunk, so the inner while loop can still execute another iteration (checking !this.ended) before the outer while loop re-checks ended.

title: decoder.close() error in feedDecoder is not caught and will propagate as an unhandled rejection from the Promise.all
description: When inputClosed becomes true, feedDecoder calls `await decoder.close()` with no surrounding try/catch. If decoder.close() throws or rejects, the error propagates up to feedDecoder, then to Promise.all in run(), which enters the catch block and calls failSession('Internal', ...). This path is actually handled correctly by the outer try/catch in run(). However, there is a subtle issue: if close() rejects after the session has already been terminated (e.g., by an error event from readDecoderEvents), failSession's isTerminal guard silently absorbs it and the encoder close error is never surfaced or logged.

title: width * height multiplication can silently overflow to a float exceeding safe integer range before the Number.isFinite guard
description: JavaScript numbers are IEEE-754 doubles. width and height come from the WASM/libjxl header event and are likely u32. For extremely large values (e.g., width=2^26, height=2^26), the product 2^52 is still representable as a finite double (it is exactly 2^52) and passes Number.isFinite, but it exceeds Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (2^53-1 is the limit; the product would be at 2^52 which is safe). For width=2^27, height=2^27, product=2^54 which is still finite but no longer an exact integer. The guard fires when minOutputBytes > 1 GiB = 2^30, so any w*h > 2^30 is caught. At 2^30 pixels both dimensions fit in u16 (max ~32768 each), so actual libjxl limits make this largely theoretical — but the guard relies on libjxl not reporting absurd u32 dimensions.

title: decoder.close() has no timeout — a hung WASM decoder can stall the feedDecoder loop indefinitely
description: After all chunks are fed and inputClosed is set, feedDecoder calls `await decoder.close()` with no timeout. The encode handler has an explicit 30-second timeout on encoder.finish() via Promise.race, acknowledging that codec finalization can hang. The decode handler lacks an analogous guard on decoder.close(). If close() blocks (e.g., a WASM deadlock), feedDecoder never returns, Promise.all never resolves, and the session remains open until the worker is terminated externally.

title: WASM load error message is forwarded verbatim to main thread via decode_error, potentially leaking internal paths or stack traces
description: When getWasm() fails, the catch block does: message: `WASM module failed to load: ${String(err)}`. The err value is the raw exception from loadWasmModule or the internal timeout Error, which may include file paths (e.g., the resolved URL from new URL('../../jxl-wasm/dist/index.js', import.meta.url)), internal module names, or Node.js stack frames. This message is sent to the main thread over postMessage and may be surfaced to application-level error handlers. The same pattern appears in the encode path at line 467.

category: missing error handling — bare async void call
title: handleReleaseState calls onCancel with void operator but the .catch(() => undefined) is only on decode; encode uses the same pattern without verifying onCancel's promise chain
description: In handleReleaseState, decode.onCancel is called as `void decode.onCancel(...).catch(() => undefined)` — the catch is explicit. However encode.onCancel is called identically: `void encode.onCancel(...).catch(() => undefined)`. Both paths look correct at first glance. The issue is that in onCancel for DecodeHandler, disposeActiveDecoder is called with `void this.disposeActiveDecoder()` (line 223) — its promise is detached. If disposeActiveDecoder rejects (e.g., decoder.dispose throws and the internal catch somehow fails), that rejection is fully orphaned with no handler. The disposePromise.catch() inside disposeActiveDecoder only handles the decoder.dispose rejection; the Promise.resolve wrapper at line 322 means a synchronous throw from decoder.dispose would not be caught by the .catch there.

category: missing error handling in queue routing
title: flushQueuedDecodeMessages and flushQueuedEncodeMessages propagate any synchronous throw from handler methods, crashing the IIFE
description: flushQueuedDecodeMessages iterates queued messages and calls routeToDecodeHandler, which calls handler methods like onChunk, onClose, onPause, onResume. These methods are synchronous and generally safe, but onCancel is called via `void handler.onCancel(msg.reason)` so its async path is detached. If any synchronous handler method throws (e.g., onChunk's failSession path throws because callbacks.onSessionEnd throws), the exception propagates out of the for-loop in flushQueuedDecodeMessages, out of the start IIFE, which has a .catch that re-sends a decode_error. So the error is eventually caught, but the session state may be partially corrupted (some queued messages processed, some not), and the decode_error may be a misleading 'Internal' error rather than the real cause.

category: missing error boundary — ChunkRing.grow silent OOM
title: ChunkRing.grow() allocates a new Array without catching allocation failure, silently propagating RangeError on OOM
description: grow() does `new Array<ArrayBuffer | undefined>(cap)` where cap doubles each time. In a memory-constrained worker, this can throw a RangeError. The exception propagates out of push(), out of onChunk(), and is not caught — onChunk is called synchronously from the message handler and has no try/catch. The RangeError would become an unhandled exception in the message handler, triggering the worker's globalThis 'error' event listener which posts a worker_error. This is recovered but the session is silently abandoned without a decode_error being sent for the specific session.

title: Error codes are bare strings with no enum/const registry, making exhaustiveness checking and client-side routing impossible
description: failSession is called with code values like 'Internal', 'InvalidInput', 'QueueOverflow', 'CapabilityMissing' — plain string literals scattered across decode-handler, encode-handler, and worker. There is no shared const/enum defining the full set. Callers on the main thread cannot switch on codes exhaustively, and a typo in a code string (e.g., 'Capability Missing' with a space) produces a valid string that compiles fine but is unrecognized client-side. A shared ErrorCode const union type would enable compile-time exhaustiveness.

title: No shared timeout-race utility leads to ad-hoc timeout patterns: encode has 30s finish timeout, decode has none on close
description: The encode handler implements a Promise.race timeout on encoder.finish() inline (lines 354-371 of encode-handler.ts). The decode handler has no equivalent on decoder.close(). A shared raceWithTimeout(promise, ms, errorMsg) helper would centralize the pattern, make it easy to add the missing decode close timeout, and prevent future omissions. The current asymmetry is a latent reliability gap.

category: missing error handling — postMessage during shutdown
title: doShutdown() calls Promise.allSettled on pendingDecodeStarts values snapshot but new starts registered after the snapshot are not waited for
description: doShutdown() snapshots pendingDecodeStarts.values() at the time of the allSettled call. Because shuttingDown=true is set before the await, handleDecodeStart checks (abortedStarts.delete || shuttingDown) and returns early — so new starts cannot enqueue after shuttingDown=true. However, a start IIFE that was already executing (past the shuttingDown check on an older message) may not have registered its promise in pendingDecodeStarts yet (it registers BEFORE the await but the IIFE body runs asynchronously). In theory, a start IIFE that is between register-in-pending and clearing-pending when doShutdown fires could have its promise included. The logic appears correct but is complex enough that a race between the IIFE epoch checks and the doShutdown snapshot could leave an in-flight handler that gets its onCancel called after decodeSessions has already been cleared, causing a no-op cancel with no session-end callback.

category: missing error handling — test helper waitFor timeout message
title: waitFor timeout message gives no context about which predicate failed, making test failures hard to diagnose
description: The test-only waitFor helper throws 'timed out waiting for handler' with no information about which test or which condition failed. In a parallel test run, multiple handlers could time out producing identical error messages with no stacktrace beyond the waitFor call. Adding predicate.toString() or a caller-supplied label to the timeout message would significantly reduce debugging time.

category: missing error boundary — wasm-loader probe response body leak
title: Diagnostic probe in loadWasmModule reads response on HEAD 405 but does not cancel or consume the body if cancel() itself throws
description: When the HEAD probe returns 405, the code falls back to a full GET: `resp = await fetchImpl(wasmUrl, ...); await resp.body?.cancel()`. If resp.body.cancel() throws (e.g., the stream is already closed or the network drops mid-cancel), the exception propagates out of the inner try block into the outer catch which silently discards it (`catch { // Probe failure is non-fatal }`). This is handled correctly by the outer catch. However, if cancel() succeeds but the body was already partially consumed (bytes already downloaded), those bytes are wasted. The deeper issue: this diagnostic code runs a full GET of a potentially large WASM file just to check availability — any network or size limit for this fallback path is absent.

title: Worker-level error events post worker_error with string-only messages; no structured context (sessionId, stack, timestamp) is included
description: The unhandledrejection and error listeners on self construct a worker_error message with only a string message field. When an internal handler bug fires an unhandled rejection, the receiving main thread gets a code ('UnhandledRejection') and a message string but no sessionId, no stack trace, and no timestamp, making post-mortem debugging of production failures very difficult. Adding at minimum a stack field (event.error?.stack) and a timestamp would dramatically improve observability.
